sentences.text: --grandmother.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
After we left Umschlagplatz, we didn't have a place to stay.
My older-- my older brother of my mother, Uncle Joseph was working for his school friend, a German, in some kind of place where they did fabricate German-- German uniforms.
And he tried to help my mother and me.
And I remember that once he-- when he was putting me, I was covered by German uniforms.
Hiding.
And I was hungry and unhappy.
And Germans were just going and looking at their uniforms.
And if I will just move, I could be killed.
I had-- I-- I was always hiding, even in-- being in ghetto.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, my mother left the ghetto with me.
And when we left ghetto, there was a man who was blackmailing her right away, [LAUGHS] and she had to-- to pay something for not going back to-- to ghetto again.
And there was waiting Mrs.
Maria who introduced herself as my grandmother.
And we were already on the-- in Warsaw, not in ghetto side.
Well, after the war, a long time after, I found out that she was a lover of my grandfather.
And--
Maternal.
The one who died.
The one who jumped off the building.
No--
His-- his wife jumped--
--out of the-- of the building.
And he died of typhus.
Miss Maria, she loved my--
She replaced the grandmother who jumped-- jumped out of the window.
She loved my grandfather, and had a child-- illegitimate child with him, who, at this time, it was something very embarrassing for a Catholic Polish woman.
And she gave this child-- but I found out that after the war-- she gave her son to some peasants who brought him up.
And nobody knew about that she has a child.
And apparently, before the war started, when my mother was married, they had a child, Uncle Stach came to my mother and told her that he's-- because my father told me about it.
And he told my mother that he's her brother, in a way.
And he needed money or help, and my mother was crying because she didn't-- didn't-- didn't want maybe to know that my grandfather had an affair.
Or maybe it was-- I don't know if-- if it was when he was married, my grandfather or before.
But she helped this-- this-- and kept contact with this man.
And this woman, Mrs.
Maria, as my mother write to my father in letters to [?
Auflag ?] would do anything to help her.
And me.
Well, she took us to home of her son, who had two children, a boy and a girl who was my age.
A little bit younger.
And this was in '42, probably end of '42.
Because everything started to really go bad after my grandmother killed herself.
It was July '42.
And I-- I think it was still '42 when I couldn't really stay in place of my uncle and to be with his children because, first of all, I looked very Jewish, and everybody could see I am a Jewish kid.
And second, my mother was well-known.
Everybody in Warsaw and everybody could recognize her.
But we got papers with the name Piechoczynska.
And I was Teresa Piechoczynska.
They had some kind of certificate because-- for Teresa Piechoczynska because they had a daughter who died and she was Teresa Piechoczynska.
So this-- my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went with us to a place by train-- I hated trains-- at night to Czestochowa, another city which nobody would know us.
And it was-- it was cold.
So it probably was end of '42.
And my feet became frostbitten.
And I-- I always remember people after us saying that we are Jewish.
And they demanded-- in the middle of night in our hotel, some guy came.
And there was another couple, and they admitted they are Jews, and they gave this guy money and they were taken away.
And I remember when I was lying in bed and my mother was screaming at the guy, saying she's not Jewish.
And he was insisting she was Jewish.
And there was a lot of blackmail going on.
And it was very dangerous to go on the street.
I remember we once went to a place, and-- to a-- an apartment to live, and the guy said that the door are close and he's going to Gestapo to give us up.
I forgot how we escaped, but I am afraid.
[LAUGHS] I believe that it was bad when you go someplace and a cat crosses your-- [LAUGHS] your street, because that what happened.
And then this guy didn't want to let us go and tell that he knows we are Jewish.
And [SIGHS] well, my grandma-- Polish grandma-- of course told me about Jesus Christ.
And--
Yes.
Miss Maria, yes.
And about Jesus Christ and how to pray and-- and how to pray for my grandfather.
And I always tried to remind her that I would rather pray for both of them.
So I knew a lot of praying at that time.
I guess it-- I had good memory as a child.
And I-- I wouldn't ever admit that I wasn't Teresa Piechoczynska.
I was-- it came naturally.
I don't know.
I never mentioned my grandmother to my mother.
I understood the situation.
I knew I-- it's better if I don't look at people and had my eyes fixed on the floor because they look less Jewish.
And everything was kind of natural.
But one day when-- in the winter, some older woman was walking on the street and she fell on the ice and my mother helped her.
And when this woman find out that my mother just need place to sleep, she took her to her home.
And my mother was sick at the time.
And lied in bed.
She had high fever.
And we had some bread and salt.
And somebody just looked at me.
I think that they were Jewish people who were working-- they were coming from ghetto and back and working on the [?
Irish-- ?] [?
Eriska ?] they say, side.
And he looked at me and he said, oh, this is Yiddish kid.
And there was a woman who heard that, and she went to Gestapo.
And next thing was Gestapo came and took my mother from her bed and me for Gestapo.
Put me in dark cellar, dark room.
And I mention bread and salt because I took bread and salt with me, because after Umschlagplatz experience, I knew that you can really get without any food, and I remember that.
And I was separately from my mother and Gestapo.
And they were coming to me and trying to find out that I am Jewish.
And I remember that I was in the middle of the room, on my knees, praying all this prayers Mrs.
Maria, my grandmother-- new grandmother told me, and saying I am not Jewish.
And I have nothing to do with Jews-- Jews.
And some salt fell on this-- on the floor.
And he-- the guy said in Polish-- I don't know they were Gestapo, but they spoke Polish.
I don't know German.
He said, you see, that's the proof you are Jewish, because that's Jewish saliva.
And I never forget it, because I went-- went there and said, don't you see that it's salt, not saliva.
I thought that must be really awful to be Jewish, you know.
Obnoxious.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the end, when they said if you will not tell us-- they were leaving me alone and then they were coming back.
And they were leaving me alone and they were coming back.
And I could hear them shooting.
I was hearing some other shooting.
And they say, your mother is still alive, but if you will not tell us all the truth, in five minutes, she will be killed.
And I told them that I am Alexandra Mayzel.
That my father is a Polish officer.
And I am not Teresa Piechoczynska and I don't want my mother to be killed.
That was in Czestochowa.
And where we didn't know anybody, and Mrs.
Maria went to Warsaw at the time.
So they took me to my mother, and I remember my mother was lying on the floor.
She was very-- they really beat her up terribly.
And they said to her and to me to get up.
And some guy was taking us through empty streets which were leading to ghetto, but one-- and he said, [GERMAN], which means stand up.
Don't move.
So we say now he will shoot us.
We were looking, he was just I think making pee.
Urinating.
They took us to Czestochowa ghetto.
I don't know.
I know it was-- it was dark.
It was night time when they were taking us to ghetto.
My mother pretended she doesn't know German, so she could hear that they say, well, there will be-- very soon they will take all these-- all them to concentration-- to the camp.
Especially children.
So they-- they-- they didn't kill us.
But they took us to-- to ghetto.
Which we didn't know anybody in Czestochowa ghetto.
But they had the little place when the doctor was working and my mother went to the little ghetto hospital.
Where did you stay when you were in the ghetto?
I stayed with the physician, who-- him and his wife, when she was in the hospital.
They were good to me.
In ghetto.
Well, she was sick in the hospital.
And I remember coming to hospital, and she had bruises which were very big all over her body.
And she told me, again, that's not-- that's not bruises.
That's an ointment that looks so dark.
And I knew better, but I never knew how to-- how to face my-- confront my mother lying to me.
So I never was able to say anything about it.
I-- I was coming to her and I was with her a lot.
And at that time when she left the hospital, we got a place.
And that was like, in one room, there were like 15 people living.
[LAUGHS] It was-- you know, there was like terrible.
And there was one girl whom-- whom I became friends with.
I remember her name-- [?
Inke ?].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't remember what was my day like.
Well, sanitation was bad.
We all had lice [LAUGHS] in-- in hair and in-- in clothes and every place.
And-- and oh, I remember.
I-- Oh, yes.
The German was taking people again to Umschlagplatz.
And they took us in Czestochowa to Umschlagplatz.
And they were taking all kids to the concentration camp.
And when my-- and that was winter.
Yes, that was winter.
But I don't know if it was '42 or '43.
I really don't know.

But I know that my mother-- probably it was '43-- my mother, when she came to this Umschlagplatz, didn't give me where other kids went to the train, but directed me to a place where women were standing.
And next there were group of women in one corner of Umschlagplatz.
There was group of men on another.
And all kids were going straight to the train.
And I was lying on the snow.
My mother told me not to move.
And women were upset with me because they gave their-- her-- their children.
And they started to really kick me and telling my mother you better take it, because we don't want to be all killed.
And all of the sudden from the group of men, one guy came with the gun and start-- wanted to show the Germans, the Nazis.
And he's-- the gun, didn't really, it wasn't good gun.
He couldn't shot.
So he put-- threw the gun into the group of other men, and started to beat the-- the main man SSS, or whatever.
So right away, a lot of Germans came to this place.
And they were putting all these Jewish men and shooting them one after another.
And looking every place and checking where the-- who has gun.
And they were so busy with looking for a gun that my mother used the situation.
She always was coping with being very kind of calm and didn't show fear.
So she very calmly took me-- my hand and took me from the Umschlagplatz and we left.
And I remember that this physician who was treating her was there, and I was the luckiest kid.
I was the only child who survived this Umschlagplatz.
He was so happy to see me alive.
And-- but I was-- after that I was sick.
I don't know.
[LAUGHS] I don't know.
Maybe this book which I showed to me which my cousin of my mother wrote.
I think that I wanted to show you data about statistics, because I really-- I still was a kid.
How old I was in '43?
Yeah, so I-- I cannot tell you things like that.
I was frightened, I was sick.
I became-- I became very sick.
I-- I, my-- Something was with kidney.
I had fever.
I was lying in bed.
Or if-- if there was a bed.
I had urine incontinence.
I even couldn't eat.
Because I was lying on-- on this ice around the snow so long.
Well, somehow my mother managed to-- to leave ghetto again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she were in different places, which I don't remember so well.
But I know that she had to pay for everything.
And I was not longer going out.
She gave me to one woman to where I was hiding in her home.
And I had to be very, very quiet, because downstairs was the woman who worked for Nazis, Volksdeutsche.
And my mother left to Warsaw-- I was in Czestochowa-- because she had to do something to get some money.
And that was the time, in '43, when was ghetto uprising.
I don't know what month it was.
Maybe it was April '43.
I just don't ever-- I don't like to read too much literature about what happened, because it's-- it was-- brings-- maybe, I don't know.
It brings memories.
Even I don't escape from my memories, but I don't read the literature.
I stay away from it.
It was the time when ghetto in Warsaw was burning and fighting, and my mother couldn't return on time to give this woman money.
And she needed money, really.
And she was saying, well, what should I do?
Your mother is not here.
I need money.
And so she figure out that she will send me back to ghetto.
And she sent me with a group of men who came to Polish-- to Irish-- how do you say, Aryan-- Aryan side.
Aryan?
Aryan?
Aryan side to work.
And she put me with them.
She didn't give me to Gestapo.
She just sent me to ghetto.
Yeah.
Well, when somebody was there.
Otherwise, I had to just stay and not to move.
[LAUGHS] Be very quiet so nobody knows I am there.
I doubt.
No, she feed me something.
I don't remember that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember coming back to ghetto alone.
And I knew that some people, like this [?
Inke's ?] and some kids.
And it was very difficult at this time.
I was really alone, and Germans were really liquidating ghetto and were taking people away and killing people and kids were pretty--
That's first time when I found about sex and I somehow started-- had the feeling that it was obnoxious and maybe that was the reason that all Jews were killed.
Because once I remember, when I was in a hotel, not in ghetto, some woman was starting to ask me how do children come from?
And I say I think they come from the stomach.
They make an operation.
Because I never really thought about it.
And this woman said, you must be Jewish.
Because if-- we all know that this certain bird is bringing children, you know, and he takes them from the water and some-- some bunch of baloney.
But it came to my mind that Jews must be different if they are really punished so terrible.
And when kids were telling me, and-- and when I found out about sex, [LAUGHS] I somehow connected that that must be only Jews.
Well anyway, it was terrible to be there.
And I thought my mother will never come, and I will die.
I stayed with different people.
One day I stayed in this place, another day I stayed with another place.
Some people liked me.
I-- I danced.
I was entertaining.
I liked to be liked.
And-- and then my mother showed up and [?
Inke's ?] mother was also on another side.
And I remember that her mother didn't come.
And she was very disappointed.
And I was so happy to see my mother, and I was kind of-- felt really bad for her.
Also what I remember what I did in Czestochowa ghetto when I was alone, I liked to sit next to the Ukrainian guy who was guardian of the ghetto.
There were like kind of wires around.
You couldn't walk through it.
Because he was singing beautiful songs.
He was walking back and forth, [LAUGHS] and I liked to listen to his songs.
That I remember.
Well, some sad.
Very sad, the Russian songs.
So I never figured out how my mother did make it back and forth from ghettos.
One--
Czestochowa ghetto.
Then when I was alone, my mother came from Warsaw to Czestochowa.
Found out from this woman that she sent me to ghetto.
In Czestochowa.
She came to Czestochowa ghetto.
That was second time Czestochowa ghetto.
And took me out.
And I don't remember how long ago-- how long I was in Czestochowa, but I remember being on the train, again, to Warsaw and being in few different places hiding.
And one of these places was the prostitute.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was-- the woman was a prostitute, and she was sleeping with German soldiers.
[LAUGHS] And she had another guy, a Jewish guy, who lived with her in her place.
So she had somehow secure her future.
In case of Nazis win, she will be having Nazis boyfriends.
And in case it will be different, she was helping some Jews for money.
But this woman was really bad to me-- for me.
She shaved my hair and--
Because I had lice.
I had scabies in her place.
I did sleep on-- there was no bed, of course.
There was a little hole in the wall, and there was a little bit-- a lot of materials.
And--
